Statistical Foundations of IQCC

Flavio Barros and contributors

2026-07-13

Source: vignettes/statistical-foundations.Rmd
statistical-foundations.Rmd

Overview

IQCC focuses on control charts whose classical normal limits may be poorly calibrated because the monitored statistic is discrete, skewed, bounded, or strongly non-normal. The package combines three ideas:

  1. compute limits on the original measurement scale;
  2. evaluate actual false-alarm probabilities whenever possible;
  3. validate formulas against independent calculations and published examples.

This vignette summarizes the probability models behind the audited p, u, double-sampling np, and generalized-variance charts.

Binomial p chart

For

X∼Binomial⁡(n,p),p̂=X/n, X\sim\operatorname{Binomial}(n,p),\qquad \hat p=X/n,

E(p̂)=p,Var⁡(p̂)=p(1−p)n. E(\hat p)=p,\qquad \operatorname{Var}(\hat p)=\frac{p(1-p)}{n}.

The normal limits use

p±zp(1−p)n. p\pm z\sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}.

The first Cornish-Fisher correction adds

(z2−1)(1−2p)6n \frac{(z^2-1)(1-2p)}{6n}

to the normal quantile on the original p scale. For z=3z=3, this becomes 4(1−2p)/(3n)4(1-2p)/(3n).

The second corrected p chart follows the operational formula in Joekes and Barbosa (2013). This is important because a generic signed lower-tail Cornish-Fisher substitution does not reproduce the paper’s reported limits.

pchart_limits(p = 0.015, n = 20, type = "normal")
pchart_limits(p = 0.015, n = 20, type = "cf1")
pchart_limits(p = 0.015, n = 20, type = "cf2")

pchart_alpha_risk(p = 0.015, n = 20, type = "cf2")

For unequal subgroup sizes, IQCC estimates the in-control proportion using the pooled estimator

p̂0=∑iXi∑ini. \hat p_0=\frac{\sum_i X_i}{\sum_i n_i}.

Poisson u chart

Let

X∼Poisson⁡(λn),U=X/n. X\sim\operatorname{Poisson}(\lambda n),\qquad U=X/n.

Then

E(U)=λ,Var⁡(U)=λn. E(U)=\lambda,\qquad \operatorname{Var}(U)=\frac{\lambda}{n}.

The standardized skewness and excess kurtosis are

γ1=1λn,γ2=1λn. \gamma_1=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda n}},\qquad \gamma_2=\frac{1}{\lambda n}.

The first Cornish-Fisher addition on the original scale is

z2−16n. \frac{z^2-1}{6n}.

The second-order term simplifies to

z(1−z2)72nλn. \frac{z(1-z^2)}{72n\sqrt{\lambda n}}.

For z=3z=3, the upper limit is

λ+3λn+43n−13nλn. \lambda+3\sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{n}} +\frac{4}{3n} -\frac{1}{3n\sqrt{\lambda n}}.

The actual risk is evaluated under the Poisson count model, respecting strict signaling inequalities.

Double-sampling np chart

Let

D1∼Binomial⁡(n1,p),D2∼Binomial⁡(n2,p), D_1\sim\operatorname{Binomial}(n_1,p),\qquad D_2\sim\operatorname{Binomial}(n_2,p),

independently. Define

a=⌊WL⌋,b=⌊UCL1⌋+1,c=⌊UCL2⌋. a=\lfloor WL\rfloor,\qquad b=\lfloor UCL_1\rfloor+1,\qquad c=\lfloor UCL_2\rfloor.

The decision rule is:

  • accept at stage 1 when D1≤aD_1\leq a;
  • signal at stage 1 when D1≥bD_1\geq b;
  • continue when a<D1<ba<D_1<b;
  • accept at stage 2 when D1+D2≤cD_1+D_2\leq c.

The first-stage acceptance probability is

PaI(p)=P(D1≤a). P_{aI}(p)=P(D_1\leq a).

The second-stage acceptance probability is

PaII(p)=∑d1=a+1b−1P(D1=d1)P(D2≤c−d1). P_{aII}(p)= \sum_{d_1=a+1}^{b-1} P(D_1=d_1)P(D_2\leq c-d_1).

Thus

PT(p)=PaI(p)+PaII(p),ARL(p)=11−PT(p). P_T(p)=P_{aI}(p)+P_{aII}(p), \qquad ARL(p)=\frac{1}{1-P_T(p)}.

The probability of requiring a second sample is

P2(p)=P(a<D1<b), P_2(p)=P(a<D_1<b),

and

ASS(p)=n1+n2P2(p). ASS(p)=n_1+n_2P_2(p). 

prob <- dsnp_prob_accept(
  p = 0.005,
  n1 = 34,
  n2 = 162,
  wl = 1.5,
  ucl1 = 2.5,
  ucl2 = 4.5
)

arl0 <- dsnp_arl(0.005, 34, 162, 1.5, 2.5, 4.5)$arl
arl1 <- dsnp_arl(0.0075, 34, 162, 1.5, 2.5, 4.5)$arl
ass0 <- dsnp_ass(0.005, 34, 162, 1.5, 2.5)$ass

The published values are approximately

ARL0=803.41,ARL1=193.22,ASS0=35.94. ARL_0=803.41,\qquad ARL_1=193.22,\qquad ASS_0=35.94.

Generalized variance chart

Let SS be the sample covariance matrix from a p-variate normal subgroup of size nn. Then

(n−1)S∼Wp(n−1,Σ). (n-1)S\sim W_p(n-1,\Sigma).

The monitored statistic is

G=|S|. G=|S|.

Bartlett’s decomposition gives

G=d|Σ|(n−1)p∏j=1pYj,Yj∼χn−j2, G\overset{d}{=} \frac{|\Sigma|}{(n-1)^p} \prod_{j=1}^{p}Y_j, \qquad Y_j\sim\chi^2_{n-j},

with independent factors.

Consequently,

E(Gr)=|Σ|r(2n−1)pr∏j=1pΓ(r+(n−j)/2)Γ((n−j)/2). E(G^r)= |\Sigma|^r \left(\frac{2}{n-1}\right)^{pr} \prod_{j=1}^{p} \frac{\Gamma(r+(n-j)/2)}{\Gamma((n-j)/2)}.

These moments provide the mean, variance, skewness, and kurtosis required by normal and Cornish-Fisher limits.

gv_limits(
  n = 10,
  p = 2,
  det_sigma = 0.5320,
  type = "normal"
)

gv_limits(
  n = 10,
  p = 2,
  det_sigma = 0.5320,
  type = "cf"
)

gv_limits(
  n = 10,
  p = 2,
  det_sigma = 0.5320,
  type = "exact"
)

For dimension two, the exact distribution simplifies to

G=d|Σ|4(n−1)2(χ2n−42)2. G\overset{d}{=} \frac{|\Sigma|}{4(n-1)^2} \left(\chi^2_{2n-4}\right)^2.

This gives exact quantiles and exact false-alarm probabilities.

For higher dimensions, IQCC offers simulation using the Bartlett factors. The package does not claim a generic Meijer-G implementation. Published exact quantiles are used only in explicitly supported dimension-three cases.

When |Σ||\Sigma| is unknown, the package estimates it from the average Phase I covariance matrix. If mm subgroups are used and ν=m(n−1)\nu=m(n-1), the determinant correction is

b3=∏j=1p(ν−j+1)νp,|Σ|̂=|S‾|b3. b_3=\frac{\prod_{j=1}^{p}(\nu-j+1)}{\nu^p}, \qquad \widehat{|\Sigma|}=\frac{|\bar S|}{b_3}.

Validation strategy

IQCC combines:

  • algebraic property tests;
  • exact or independently coded numerical oracles;
  • reproduction of published numerical examples.

This strategy is particularly important for control charts because a plausible formula may still use a different tail convention, integer threshold, or parameter-estimation rule than the intended method.

References

  • Joekes, S. and Barbosa, E. P. (2013). An improved attribute control chart for monitoring non-conforming proportion in high quality processes. Control Engineering Practice, 21, 407-412.
  • Joekes, S., Smrekar, M. and Barbosa, E. P. (2015). Extending a double sampling control chart for non-conforming proportion in high quality processes to the case of small samples. Statistical Methodology, 23, 35-49.
  • Barbosa, E. P., Gneri, M. A. and Meneguetti, A. Improving Shewhart-type Generalized Variance Control Charts for Multivariate Process Variability Monitoring using Cornish-Fisher Quantile Correction, Meijer-G Function and Other Tools.

On this page

Developed by Flavio Barros.
